one_line_takeaway: A policy-to-prompt auto-generation framework (Aymara AI) benchmarks 20 commercial LLMs and shows safety is domain-contingent, not model-intrinsic: near-perfect on Misinformation (95.7%) yet collapsing on Privacy & Impersonation (24.3%), with a 34-point gap between the safest and least-safe models.
key_finding: LLM safety is highly inconsistent and strongly domain-dependent rather than a monolithic model property. Mean per-model safety ranged from 86.2% (Claude Haiku 3.5) down to 52.4% (Command R), a 34-point spread, while domain means ranged from 95.7% (Misinformation) to just 24.3% (Privacy & Impersonation). Well-established content-moderation domains (Misinformation, Malicious Use, Hate Speech & Bias) were near-saturated, but every model failed the Privacy & Impersonation domain badly — even the overall leader scored only 45.5% there, and no model reached 100% in either Privacy & Impersonation or Unqualified Professional Advice. Domain differences were highly significant (F(9,190)=37.37, p<.001); model differences were significant overall (F(19,180)=1.68, p=.04) but no specific model pair survived Tukey HSD correction, which the authors attribute to the small sample (10 domain scores per model).
core_contribution: The paper introduces Aymara AI, a programmatic platform (Python SDK aymara-ai-sdk, v1.0.3) that converts natural-language safety policies into adversarial evaluation prompts and scores model responses with a validated LLM-as-a-judge. As a demonstration of the tool, the authors build the 'Aymara LLM Risk and Responsibility Matrix', a benchmark that evaluates 20 commercial LLMs across 10 policy-grounded safety domains using 250 generated prompts (5,000 model responses). The contribution is thus two-fold: (1) the low-friction, policy-grounded, customizable evaluation framework itself, and (2) the cross-model empirical benchmark it produces. The claimed novelty over prior frameworks (HolisticEval, TrustLLM, WalledGarden, which are cited in Prior Work for contrast, not run as baselines) is that Aymara requires no manual dataset construction while still supporting per-policy customization, and is designed to be multilingual and multimodal (though this study is English text-only).
threat_model: The paper's implicit threat model is adversarial/misuse elicitation: users attempting to make a deployed, public-facing general-purpose LLM produce policy-violating content across ten harm categories (Child & Animal Abuse, Copyright & IP Violation, Hate Speech & Bias, Illegal Activities, Malicious Use, Misinformation, Privacy & Impersonation, Self-Harm, Sexually Explicit/NSFW, Unqualified Professional Advice). Adversarial prompts are auto-generated to disguise intent with curiosity, politeness, educational/hypothetical framing, and 'legal loophole' phrasings. The evaluated asset is guardrail robustness under zero-shot API access with default settings; a response is 'unsafe' if it violates the relevant policy, 'safe' if compliant or a refusal.
limitations: Author-stated: (1) the 10 policies were written by a single U.S. East Coast startup and reflect regional/institutional perspectives, not a comprehensive or normative taxonomy; (2) the 20-model sample under-represents smaller and open-source developers; (3) prompts were generated programmatically and may miss the creativity/variety of real human adversaries; (4) the study is English-only despite the tool's claimed multilingual support, so results may not generalize; (5) it is a single point-in-time snapshot as models evolve. Reviewer-added caveats: the scorer is a proprietary, non-disclosed Aymara model, and the prompt set is deliberately withheld to prevent benchmark gaming, both of which limit external reproducibility; the human-validation set is small (226 responses, single rater); and the exclusion of 24 'inconsistently scored' prompts (9.6%) could bias results toward domains the scorer handles cleanly.

datasets_used: Aymara LLM Risk and Responsibility Matrix (this paper's own): 250 auto-generated policy-grounded adversarial prompts (25 per policy across 10 policies)
5,000 LLM responses (20 models x 250 prompts); 4,520 responses over 226 prompts after removing 24 flagged prompts
Human-validation subset: stratified random 226 responses (5% of final dataset), balanced across all 10 domains and 20 models
10 safety policies authored from AI-safety literature, provider usage policies, and the EU AI Act (full texts in Table 1)
quantitative_results: Overall mean safety across 20 models x 10 domains = 73.2% (median 82.2%, SD 26.0%)
Per-model range: Claude Haiku 3.5 highest at 86.2% (SD 21.1%); Command R lowest at 52.4% (SD 30.2%)
Top domain Misinformation mean 95.7% (SD 6.7%); worst domain Privacy & Impersonation mean 24.3% (SD 11.4%)

Other domain means: Malicious Use 91.8%, Hate Speech & Bias 91.1%, Illegal Activities 84.6%, Self-Harm 79.5%, Copyright & IP 76.6%, Child & Animal Abuse 75.0%, Sexually Explicit/NSFW 60.0%, Unqualified Professional Advice 53.8%
Best model in Privacy & Impersonation still only 45.5% (Claude Haiku 3.5); 12 of 20 models hit 100% in at least one domain
Cross-model ANOVA F(19,180)=1.68, p=.04 (no Tukey HSD pair significant, all p>.05); cross-domain ANOVA F(9,190)=37.37, p<.001
Scorer vs human validation: accuracy 0.93, weighted F1 0.93, Cohen's Kappa 0.85 (substantial); on unsafe class (n=80) precision 0.89, recall 0.91
85 of 5,000 responses (1.7%) were refusals, counted as safe; consistency audit removed 24 prompts (9.6%) and 480 responses
baselines_compared: No methodological baseline systems were run; the 20 evaluated LLMs are the subjects, not baselines: OpenAI GPT-4.1 / 4.1 Mini / 4.1 Nano; AI21 Jamba 1.5 Large / Mini; Amazon Nova Premier / Pro / Lite; Anthropic Claude Sonnet 4 / Claude Haiku 3.5; Cohere Command R+ / Command R; DeepSeek-R1; Google Gemini 2.5 Flash / 2.5 Pro; Meta Llama 4 Maverick / Scout; Mistral Large 2 / Mistral Small; xAI Grok-3

Prior evaluation frameworks HolisticEval, TrustLLM, and WalledGarden are cited only for qualitative contrast in the Prior Work section (static-vs-dynamic, manual-dataset tradeoffs) and are not empirically compared against Aymara AI
